camera_comp.name: Camera COMP
camera_comp.sum: Defines the 3D viewpoint and projection for a Render TOP. Controls position, orientation, FOV, near/far clip, and look-at target. Referenced by Render TOP via path parameter.
tips: The Camera COMP is referenced by path in the Render TOP Camera parameter — it is NOT wire-connected.
Drive Translate and Rotate parameters from CHOP expressions for smooth animated camera movement: op('null_cam')['tx']
Use the Look At parameter to automatically orient the camera toward a target object without manually computing rotation.
Set Projection to Orthographic for 2D-style renders, data visualisations, and isometric views.
FOV (Field of View) controls the lens angle. Narrow FOV (telephoto, ~20-30 degrees) compresses depth; wide FOV (fisheye, ~90-120 degrees) exaggerates depth.
Near Clip and Far Clip define the visible depth range. Objects closer than Near or farther than Far are clipped. Keep the ratio Far/Near below 10000 for good depth buffer precision.
For VR-style 360 rendering, use two cameras (left eye, right eye) with appropriate eye separation and each rendering to its own Render TOP.
Use Object CHOP to extract the camera's world transform for use in CHOP-driven effects.
A Camera COMP inside a Geometry COMP or other COMP hierarchy inherits the parent's transform — useful for attaching cameras to animated objects.
For cinematic depth-of-field, render to RGBA32Float format and post-process the depth channel from a Render Pass TOP.
ex:
import math

def onFrameStart(frame):
    t = absTime.seconds
    cam = op('cam1')

    radius = 5
    speed = 0.3  # radians per second

    cam.par.tx = math.sin(t * speed) * radius
    cam.par.tz = math.cos(t * speed) * radius
    cam.par.ty = 1.5  # slightly above ground

    # Face the origin
    cam.par.lookat = 'geo1'  # or set manually
    # Alternatively compute angle:
    angle = math.degrees(math.atan2(cam.par.tx, cam.par.tz))
    cam.par.ry = angle + 180

import math

def onFrameStart(frame):
    t = absTime.seconds
    cam = op('cam1')

    # Oscillate FOV between 20 and 70 degrees
    fov = 45 + math.sin(t * 0.5) * 25
    cam.par.fov = fov

    # Counter-translate to maintain subject size (dolly zoom)
    # Subject at z=0, camera starts at z=5
    import math
    dist = 2.5 / math.tan(math.radians(fov / 2))
    cam.par.tz = dist

CAMERAS = ['cam_front', 'cam_side', 'cam_overhead', 'cam_closeup']
current_index = 0

def nextCamera():
    global current_index
    current_index = (current_index + 1) % len(CAMERAS)
    op('render1').par.camera = CAMERAS[current_index]
    print(f'[Camera] Switched to {CAMERAS[current_index]}')

# Call from a button press or MIDI note

# World-space position

tips: Set the Target TOP parameter to a Null TOP that is DOWNSTREAM of the Feedback TOP — targeting an upstream node breaks the loop.
Use pixel format RGBA16Float on the Feedback TOP to prevent precision loss and colour banding during decay.
Lower the Level TOP Brightness to 0.97–0.99 between Feedback and target to create a slow trail decay without full persistence.
Add a Transform TOP (Scale X/Y = 1.005, Rotate = 0.1) between Feedback and target for the classic zoom-rotate spiral.
Use op('feedback1').par.resetpulse.pulse() from a script to clear the buffer instantly to black.
Set Bypass Feedback to 1 via parameter expression or Python to temporarily pass through the source image without feedback.
Inserting a GLSL TOP between Feedback and target allows per-pixel shader manipulation of the feedback buffer each frame.
Use a Composite TOP with Operand=Add to inject new source material into the loop: Feedback output -> Composite input 0, source -> Composite input 1, Composite -> Null target.
Match the Feedback TOP resolution to the filter chain resolution to avoid unexpected rescaling.
Feedback TOP with Transform SOP chained into a Render TOP creates pseudo-3D layered depth effects.

# Set the Target TOP parameter
op('feedback1').par.top = 'null1'

# Bypass feedback (passthrough mode)
op('feedback1').par.reset = 1

# Re-enable feedback
op('feedback1').par.reset = 0

# Pulse reset to clear buffer
op('feedback1').par.resetpulse.pulse()

# In an Execute DAT - onFrameStart
def onFrameStart(frame):
    # Get audio amplitude from Null CHOP reference
    amp = op('null_audio')['chan1']
    # Base decay 0.95, pulse to 0.99 on loud hits
    decay = 0.95 + amp * 0.04
    op('level1').par.brightness = decay

# In a Script DAT or Execute DAT

    top = op('feedback1')
    # numpyArray requires Script TOP or CUDA access;

    print(f'Feedback resolution: {top.width}x{top.height}')
    print(f'Pixel format: {top.pixelFormat}')

import math

def onFrameStart(frame):
    t = absTime.seconds
    geo = op('geo1')

    # Circular orbit
    radius = 3
    geo.par.tx = math.sin(t * 0.5) * radius
    geo.par.tz = math.cos(t * 0.5) * radius

    # Continuous Y rotation
    geo.par.ry = (t * 45) % 360  # 45 degrees per second

# In a Script CHOP — generates N instances on a sphere surface
import numpy as np

N = 500  # number of instances

def cook(scriptOp):
    scriptOp.clear()
    tx = scriptOp.appendChan('tx')
    ty = scriptOp.appendChan('ty')
    tz = scriptOp.appendChan('tz')
    tx.setSize(N)
    ty.setSize(N)
    tz.setSize(N)

    golden = (1 + np.sqrt(5)) / 2
    indices = np.arange(N)
    theta = np.arccos(1 - 2 * (indices + 0.5) / N)
    phi   = 2 * np.pi * indices / golden
    r = 5  # sphere radius

    for i in range(N):
        tx[i] = r * np.sin(theta[i]) * np.cos(phi[i])
        ty[i] = r * np.cos(theta[i])
        tz[i] = r * np.sin(theta[i]) * np.sin(phi[i])

# Geometry COMP: Instance On = 1, Instance CHOP = 'script1'

line_metrics_pop.sum: You can add metrics attributes to Line Strips. They can be created as point attribute or the vertex attributes.

line_pop.name: Line POP

line_smooth_pop.sum: The Line Smooth POP smooths line strips by blending the positions of adjacent points along a line strip.

logic_chop.sum: The Logic CHOP first converts channels of all its input CHOPs into binary (0 = off, 1 = on) channels and then combines the channels using a variety of logic operations.

lookup_dat.sum: The Lookup DAT  outputs values from a lookup Table. The first input is an index into the second input.

ltc_in_chop.sum: The LTC In CHOP reads SMPTE timecode data encoded into an audio signal. Read the wikipedia overview Linear Timecode.

ltc_out_chop.name: LTC Out CHOP
ltc_out_chop.sum: The LTC Out CHOP outputs "linear timecode" which is a SMPTE timecode data encoded into an audio signal. See also Linear Timecode.

magnet_sop.sum: The Magnet SOP allows you to affect deformations of the input geometry with another object using a "magnetic field" of influence, defined by a metaball field. It allows the creation of animated bumps

material_sop.name: Material SOP
material_sop.sum: The Material SOP allows the assignment of materials (MATs) to geometry at the SOP level.

math_chop.name: Math CHOP
math_chop.sum: The Math CHOP performs arithmetic operations on channels. The channels of a CHOP can be combined into one channel, and several CHOPs can be combined into one CHOP.
tips: Use the Range page to remap values between different ranges (e.g., 0-1 to -1 to +1).
The Combine CHOPs section lets you add, subtract, multiply or average multiple input channels.
Set 'Match By' to 'Channel Name' when combining CHOPs with different channel orders.
Use Multiply post-operation to scale values quickly.
ex:
# Create Math CHOP for range mapping
math1 = op('/project1').create(mathCHOP)
math1.name = 'remap'

# Set range mapping
math1.par.fromrange1 = 0
math1.par.fromrange2 = 1
math1.par.torange1 = -180
math1.par.torange2 = 180

# Read remapped value
val = math1['chan1'][0]

Use Duplicate Names to control how channels with the same name are handled.
Great for collecting channels from different sources into a single CHOP for export.
ex:
# Reference merged channels
merge = op('merge1')
for chan in merge.chans():
    print(f'{chan.name}: {chan[0]}')

merge_dat.sum: The Merged DAT is a multi-input DAT which merges the text or tables from the input DATs together.

mirror_top.sum: Mirrors part of an image on top of itself.

tips: Use HAP codec for fastest playback performance on GPU.
Set Pre-Read Frames to buffer ahead for smoother playback.
NotchLC and HAP Q provide good quality with GPU-accelerated decoding.
Use the 'File' parameter to load image sequences with patterns like 'image$F4.png'.
ex:
# Configure Movie File In TOP
movie = op('moviefilein1')
movie.par.file = 'path/to/video.mov'
movie.par.play = True
movie.par.speed = 1.0
movie.par.index = 0.5  # Seek to 50%

# Get movie info
width = movie.width
height = movie.height

neighbor_pop.sum: The Neighbor POP uses the P attribute to find, for each point of the input, the closest points to it.  It puts the indexes of the closest points in an array attribute Nebr. You specify the max number ...

noise_chop.name: Noise CHOP
noise_chop.sum: The Noise CHOP makes an irregular wave that never repeats, with values approximately in the range -1 to +1.
tips: Use Transform parameters (Translate, Rotate) to sample different parts of noise space for unique patterns per channel.
Set Type to 'Sparse' for highest quality continuous noise; use 'Random' for white noise.
Increase Harmonics and adjust Roughness to add detail - higher roughness = bumpier noise.
The Seed parameter gives completely different patterns for the same settings - useful for variation.
Works identically with Time Slicing on/off, except Harmonic Summation and Brownian types.
ex:
# Create Noise CHOP
noise = op('/project1').create(noiseCHOP)
noise.name = 'noise1'

# Set noise type
noise.par.type = 'sparse'  # Options: sparse, hermite, harmonic, brownian, random, alligator
noise.par.seed = 42
noise.par.period = 2.0  # Period in seconds
noise.par.amp = 1.0
noise.par.harmon = 3  # Number of harmonics
noise.par.rough = 0.5  # Roughness

# Read noise values
val = noise['chan1'][0]
print(f'Current noise value: {val}')

noise_pop.name: Noise POP
noise_pop.sum: The Noise POP affects every point with a noise field, which can be thought of as smooth, randomly-rising and falling values in 3D space.

noise_sop.name: Noise SOP

normal_map_top.sum: The Normal Map TOP takes an input image and creates a normal map by finding edges in the image. This can then be used for bump mapping (See Phong MAT and PBR MAT).

null_chop.name: Null CHOP
null_chop.sum: The Null CHOP is used as a place-holder and does not alter the data coming in.
tips: Use Null CHOPs as export endpoints - this lets you swap upstream CHOP networks without re-exporting.
Set Cook Type to 'Selective' to optimize performance by only recooking downstream when values actually change.
Use as a 'breakpoint' in your network for debugging - you can inspect values without affecting the chain.
ex:
# Create Null CHOP for clean exports
null1 = op('/project1').create(nullCHOP)
null1.name = 'export_null'
null1.par.cooktype = 'selective'
null1.par.checkvalues = True

# Read values from Null CHOP
for chan in null1.chans():
    print(f'{chan.name}: {chan[0]}')

tips: Use Null DATs to create clean reference points for table data.
Like other Null operators, great for exports and organizing complex DAT networks.

null_mat.name: Null MAT
null_mat.sum: The Null MAT has no effect on the data. It is an instance of the MAT connected to its input. It doesn't do much but comes in handy when building networks.

null_pop.name: Null POP
null_pop.sum: The Null POP does nothing - it passes its input to its output unchanged. It does not consume any CPU or GPU memory.

null_sop.name: Null SOP
null_sop.sum: The Null SOP has no effect on the geometry. It is an instance of the SOP connected to its input. The Null SOP is often used when making reference to a SOP network, allowing new SOPs to be added to the

null_top.name: Null TOP
null_top.sum: The Null TOP has no effect on the image. It is an instance of the TOP connected to its input. The Null TOP is often used when making reference to a TOP network, allowing new TOPs to be added to the ne
tips: Use Null TOPs as output points for referencing textures elsewhere in your network.
Null TOPs are lightweight and don't consume extra GPU memory beyond the input texture.
Great for organizing complex networks - name your Null TOPs descriptively (e.g., 'final_output').
ex:
# Reference a Null TOP's texture
output = op('final_output')  # Null TOP
width = output.width
height = output.height

# Save screenshot
output.save('screenshot.png')

render_top.name: Render TOP
render_top.sum: Renders a 3D scene (Geometry COMPs + Camera COMP + Lights) into a 2D image. The primary bridge between the 3D COMP world and the 2D TOP world.
tips: Set the Camera parameter to a Camera COMP path, not a wire — Render TOP has no TOP inputs.
Use '*' in the Geometry parameter to automatically render all visible Geometry COMPs in the same level.
Enable Depth Buffer to allow proper depth-sorted transparency and Render Pick CHOP interaction.
Use Render Pass TOP to extract separate render buffers (colour, depth, normals, position) from a single render call.
Anti-alias the output with the Samples parameter (1 = none, 4 = 4xMSAA). Higher samples cost GPU memory.
Use multiple Render TOPs with the same scene but different cameras for split-screen or security-camera effects.
Set Pixel Format to RGBA16Float for HDR rendering that can handle values above 1.0 for bloom post-processing.
The Render TOP respects the Display flag on Geometry COMPs — turn off Display on a Geometry COMP to exclude it from the render without removing the Geometry parameter.
Wire the Render TOP output into a Level TOP to do basic tone-mapping, or into a GLSL TOP for custom post-processing.
Use Light COMPs (Point Light, Directional Light, Environment Light) to add dynamic lighting. Reference them in the Light parameter.
ex:
# Assign camera
op('render1').par.camera = 'cam1'

# Assign geometry (wildcard)
op('render1').par.geometry = '*'

# Assign specific geometry operators
op('render1').par.geometry = 'geo1 geo2'

# Read current render resolution
print(f'Render output: {op("render1").width}x{op("render1").height}')

CAMERAS = ['cam_front', 'cam_side', 'cam_top', 'cam_isometric']

def setCameraByIndex(index):
    """Switch Render TOP camera by index (0-3)."""
    idx = max(0, min(index, len(CAMERAS) - 1))
    op('render1').par.camera = CAMERAS[idx]
    print(f'[Render] Switched to {CAMERAS[idx]}')

# Call from MIDI Event DAT:
# setCameraByIndex(int(val / 32))  # Map 0-127 to 0-3

# In Execute DAT - onFrameStart
import math

def onFrameStart(frame):
    t = absTime.seconds
    r = op('render1')
    r.par.bgcolorr = (math.sin(t * 0.3) + 1) * 0.5 * 0.1
    r.par.bgcolorb = (math.cos(t * 0.2) + 1) * 0.5 * 0.1

# Toggle a Geometry COMP in/out of the render
def toggleGeo(geoName):
    geo = op(geoName)
    geo.display = not geo.display

# Example: hide particles during a scene transition
toggleGeo('geo_particles')

warp_chop.sum: The Warp CHOP time-warps the channels of the first input (the Pre-Warp Channels) using one warping channel in the second input (the Warp Curve). The Warp Curve acts as either a rate control or an inde

wave_chop.name: Wave CHOP
wave_chop.sum: The Wave CHOP makes repeating waves with a variety of shapes. It is by default 10-seconds of 1-second sine waves, a total of 600 frames. You can adjust period (frequency), phase, shape, amplitude and

web_client_dat.name: Web Client DAT
web_client_dat.sum: The Web Client DAT allows you to send HTTP requests to web servers from TouchDesigner. It supports GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS and PATCH http methods.

web_render_top.name: Web Render TOP
web_render_top.sum: The Web Render TOP takes a URL or DAT and renders a webpage via a separate browser process that uses Chromium Embedded Frameworks, and passes the result back through shared memory. It renders non-Flas

web_server_dat.name: Web Server DAT
web_server_dat.sum: The Web Server DAT allows you to connect to TouchDesigner as a web client, through a web browser for example. The Web Server DAT supports HTTP, WebSockets, as well as the sending/receiving of binary d

webrtc_dat.name: WebRTC DAT
webrtc_dat.sum: A WebRTC DAT represents a peer on one end of any number of WebRTC peer-to-peer connections.

websocket_dat.name: WebSocket DAT
websocket_dat.sum: The WebSocket DAT receives and parses WebSocket messages. WebSockets are fast an efficient two way communication protocol used by web servers and clients. Each message is parsed and appended as a row

window_comp.name: Window COMP
window_comp.sum: The Window Component allows you to create and maintain a separate floating or fixed window displaying the contents of any Panel or any Node Viewer.

wireframe_mat.name: Wireframe MAT
wireframe_mat.sum: The Wireframe MAT renders the edges of polygons and curves as lines.

wireframe_sop.name: Wireframe SOP
wireframe_sop.sum: The Wireframe SOP converts edges to tubes and points to spheres, creating the look of a wire frame structure in renderings. This is ideal for modelling tube structures and pipes.

wrnchai_chop.name: WrnchAI CHOP

xml_dat.name: XML DAT
xml_dat.sum: The XML DAT can be used to parse arbitrary XML and SGML/HTML formatted data. Once formatted, selected sections of the text can be output for further processing.
